design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate jet down infinite scrolling river, destroy enemies and fuel depots, manage fuel as primary resource constraint, earn points before crash or fuel depletion
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Fuel scarcity vs. forward progression; navigation challenge vs. combat engagement; risk/reward of destroying fuel depots for points
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Piloting a high-tech jet fighter on a dangerous solo raid behind enemy lines, executing precision strikes against enemy infrastructure
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: River Raid commits to vertical scrolling as core design constraint (horizontally scrolling felt 'jerky'), makes fuel management the primary strategic element rather than pure combat, and embraces infinite procedural progression rather than fixed endpoints. Carol Shaw's deliberate design choice to solve technical limitations through thematic coherence (river canyon visuals emerging from graph paper iteration) defines the game's focused design philosophy.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple jets; player loses one jet per collision with river banks or enemy objects; restarts at same river section or at destroyed bridge checkpoint if available
difficulty.checkpointing: Soft checkpoints at destroyed bridges; player respawns at bridge location upon subsequent life loss, encouraging bridge destruction as progression mechanism
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Hardware switch selection provides modest difficulty tuning: Switch A enables straight missiles (easier targeting), Switch B enables guided missiles (more complex but potentially more powerful). Atari 8-bit version adds bridge selection for starting position. Fuel tank depletion scales as river progresses, creating escalating difficulty curve.

progression.upgrade_system: Guided missile toggle via hardware switch; no in-game power progression
progression.unlock_mechanism: Bridge destruction unlocks checkpoint respawn locations; Atari 8-bit version allows player selection of which bridge to restart at
replayability_hooks: Infinite procedural river with no actual ending
Increasing difficulty as fuel tanks become scarcer deeper in river
Guided vs. straight missile toggle creates distinct difficulty modes

world_building_techniques: B1 StratoWing Assault Jet framing establishes military context
River of No Return naming suggests high-stakes mission
Enemy targets (tankers, helicopters, fuel depots, bridges) reinforce military infrastructure destruction theme
Canyon river geography creates implicit enemy territory setting

faction_indicators: Player controls allied B1 StratoWing fighter
Enemy forces: tankers, helicopters, bridges suggesting organized military opposition
danger_signals: River banks as hard environmental hazards
Enemy projectile fire (helicopters in Atari 8-bit version fire back)
environmental_storytelling: River canyon geography establishes behind-enemy-lines raid context
Fuel depots as enemy supply infrastructure
Bridges as strategic military targets representing troop advance points
Scrolling river progression suggests linear mission penetration into enemy territory
